title: Echanes v. Hailar
holding: Ejectment (forcible entry/unlawful detainer) determines possession de facto only; the court decides who has the better right to physical possession.

links.relevance: The summary case resolves physical possession, not title; ownership is only incidental.
links.note: Bounds what the MTC decides on Aug 12.

title: Esperal v. Trompeta-Esperal
holding: In ejectment, ownership is tackled only as an incident to possession and only if raised; the ruling on ownership is provisional and not conclusive in a separate action.

links.relevance: Any MTC ownership finding is provisional; final title questions remain for a separate action.
links.note: Supports the wedge/test-and-reserve posture (final nullity in RTC).

title: Santiago v. Northbay Knitting, Inc.
holding: The core issue in ejectment is the better right to physical/material possession; the summary character is preserved.

links.relevance: Frames the possession contest independent of ultimate ownership.

title: Mariano v. City of Naga (Decision)
holding: A donation of real property is void absent the Art. 749 formalities (public instrument, acceptance in a public document, proper notification if separate).

links.relevance: The LGU donation of MWK land is void if the Art. 749 formalities were not met.
links.note: Core void-donation authority; named in the matter theory.

title: Mariano v. City of Naga (En Banc)
holding: Where government takes private property for public use without valid conveyance or expropriation and return is no longer feasible, the remedy is just compensation (value at time of taking + legal interest); laches does not bar the registered owner's recovery.

links.relevance: Even if return is infeasible, Patricia is entitled to just compensation with interest; laches is no bar.
links.note: The remedy/outcome anchor for LGU recovery.

title: Department of Education, Culture and Sports v. Del Rosario
holding: A donation of an immovable is void absent the Art. 749 formalities; the case discusses proof issues where the deed of donation is missing.

links.relevance: Void donation absent formalities, and proof strategy when the donation deed cannot be produced.
links.note: Supports the no-valid-donation position + missing-deed proof.

title: Hocorma Foundation, Inc. v. Mabalacat Institute, Inc.
holding: Without a public instrument/notarization and proper acceptance, a donation is void; solemn contracts are perfected only on compliance with statutory formalities.

links.relevance: Recent (2025) reaffirmation that formal defects void the donation.

title: Land Bank of the Philippines v. Suntay
holding: The RTC sitting as a Special Agrarian Court has original and exclusive jurisdiction to determine just compensation under RA 6657, Sec. 57; vesting that in DARAB or reducing the SAC to appellate review is void.

links.relevance: Confirms the SAC (RTC) forum for the just-compensation determination in CV-6839.

title: Land Bank of the Philippines v. Escaro
holding: Determination of just compensation is a judicial function vested in the RTC-SAC under Sec. 57; rules reducing the SAC to an appellate body over DAR/DARAB are void.

links.relevance: Reinforces SAC original jurisdiction as a judicial function.

title: Marken, Inc. v. Land Bank of the Philippines
holding: Affirms Sec. 57: the RTC-SAC has original and exclusive jurisdiction; administrative arrangements treating SAC review as appellate are invalid.

title: Ocampo v. Batara-Sapad
holding: Guardians, whether in fact or in law, have no power to dispose of or encumber their wards' properties without a court order.

links.relevance: No disposition/encumbrance of a ward's property without prior guardianship-court approval.

title: Neri v. Heirs of Uy
holding: Any act of disposition/alienation goes beyond administration; even a natural guardian cannot dispose of or encumber a minor's property — that authority belongs only to a judicial guardian, and only with prior court approval.

links.relevance: Disposition exceeds administration; requires judicial guardian + prior court approval.

title: Navarro v. San Agustin
holding: A sale of a ward's real property by a judicial guardian without prior authority from the guardianship court is void and cannot be validated by subsequent approval from another court or by agreement among heirs.

links.relevance: A guardian's sale without prior guardianship-court authority is void and unratifiable.
links.note: Void-and-unratifiable rule; exclusive authority in the guardianship court.
